title: Hindoo Tales; Or, the Adventures of Ten Princes
description: Hindoo Tales; Or, the Adventures of Ten Princes — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "Hindoo Tales; Or, the Adventures of Ten Princes" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: Accusation in the Tales
memes.description: False accusations as a recurring plot device in the Tales, used to test character, advance intrigue, and create dramatic tension.

memes.label: Alaka
memes.description: A mountain inhabited by Kuvera and the Yakshas, listed in the index of proper names of the Daśakumāracarita.

memes.label: Alliance in the Tales
memes.description: Alliance—the binding of parties by mutual pact, often sealed by marriage—appears as a political instrument for gathering strength against a feud or a common enemy.

memes.label: Ambâlika
memes.description: Princess of Anga, daughter of King Sinhavarma, sought in marriage by Chandavarma, appearing in Chapters 7 and 11.

memes.label: Amittravarma
memes.description: King or Governor of Mahishmati, half-brother of the late king, who sought to marry his brother's widow and met his death as punishment from heaven.

memes.label: "Anantavarma"
memes.description: "Anantavarma, King of Vidarba, a young ruler of great abilities but more devoted to the arts than governance, defeated and killed by the King of Asmaka."

memes.label: Andhra
memes.description: A kingdom in southern India, ruled by a king who had long been a suitor for the hand of a princess — the setting for Mantragupta's southern adventures.

memes.label: Anga
memes.description: An eastern kingdom ruled by King Sinhavarma, father of Ambâlika, and a devoted ally of Râjahansa of Magadha.

memes.label: Apahâravarma
memes.description: One of the Ten Princes, son of Prahâravarma King of Mithila, and narrator of his own adventure in Chapter 8

memes.label: Arthapâla
memes.description: One of the Ten Princes, son of minister Kâmapâla and the Princess Kantimati

memes.label: Arthapati
memes.description: A wealthy merchant of Champâ who sought to marry Kulapâlikâ and was implicated in the theft of a magic purse

memes.label: Aryaketu
memes.description: Respected minister and loyal friend of Visruta, defending him against detractors, appearing in the tale of Chapter 25.

memes.label: Asmaka
memes.description: A kingdom ruled by a cunning and cruel king who coveted neighbouring territories and was ultimately overcome through the efforts of the ten princes.

memes.label: Assembly in the Tales
memes.description: Public assemblies—gatherings for dispute, display, and judgment—serve as recurring settings for confrontation and staged events across the tales.

memes.label: Attack in the Tales
memes.description: Attacks—whether by Bheel brigands, wild beasts, or enemy forces—are a recurring narrative event in the tales, serving as catalysts for adventure and tests of character.

memes.label: Avanti
memes.description: A major kingdom and city in ancient India, capital of King Mânasâra, home of Princess Avantisundari, and the setting of Râjavâhana's fateful encounter with his bride at the Festival of Kâma.

memes.label: Avantisundari
memes.description: Princess of Avanti, daughter of King Mânasâra, and beloved wife of Râjavâhana, the hero of the ten princes

memes.label: Balabhadra
memes.description: A merchant, husband of Ratnavati, who was tricked by a clever scheme into falling in love with his own neglected wife.

memes.label: Bâlachandrika
memes.description: Daughter of the merchant Bandhupâla, wife of Pushpodbhava, and confidante of Princess Avantisundari

memes.label: Bandhupâla
memes.description: A merchant of Mâlwa, father of Bâlachandrika, who aided travellers and used his knowledge of bird omens to gather intelligence

memes.label: Battle in the Tales
memes.description: Battles and armed conflicts form pivotal narrative events throughout the tales, driving the plot and testing the valour of princes and kings.

memes.label: Beauty
memes.description: A highly valued aesthetic quality of persons, treated in the narrative as a mark of distinction, divine favour, and narrative significance.

memes.label: Benâres
memes.description: The sacred city where Kâmapâla served as lord and the deposed King Sinhaghosha once ruled, and the setting for part of Mantragupta's adventure.

memes.label: Bhimadhanwa
memes.description: The brother of Princess Kandukavati, a persistent antagonist who persecuted his sister and was eventually subdued by her allies

memes.label: Blessing
memes.description: A spiritual benediction exchanged between characters — blessings function as a form of spiritual currency, conferring favour and gratitude.

memes.label: Brahma
memes.description: Brahma, one of the three principal gods of the Hindu pantheon, named alongside Vishnu and Siva in the narrative

memes.label: Brahman
memes.description: The priestly and learned class in Hindoo society, serving as counsellors, religious authorities, and custodians of sacred knowledge.

memes.label: Capture in the Tales
memes.description: Capture and captivity are recurring narrative events throughout the tales, driving plot twists and testing the resourcefulness of princes and kings.

memes.label: Caste
memes.description: The four-fold social hierarchy of traditional Hindu society, maintained as a mark of righteous rule.

memes.label: Ceremony in the Tales
memes.description: Religious and social ceremonies depicted in the Tales, including the Simanta pregnancy ritual and formal marriage rites.

memes.label: Champa
memes.description: A capital city on the banks of the Ganges, besieged by Chandavarma and home to the merchant Arthapati who sought to marry Kulapâlikâ.

memes.label: Chandasinha
memes.description: King, maternal grandfather of a princess, mentioned in connection with his son's untimely death.

memes.label: Chandavarma
memes.description: Regent and antagonist who besieged Champa, held the princess in his power, and was murdered by an assassin

memes.label: Chandrasena
memes.description: Foster-sister of Princess Kandukavati, beloved of Kosadâsa, who helped unite the lovers in the palace

memes.label: Charmavarma
memes.description: Son of the king's sister, appointed joint regent with his brother Dâruvarma while Darpasâra performed penance in the Himâlaya Mountains

memes.label: Compassion
memes.description: The moral sentiment of pity and active concern for the suffering of others, frequently shown by holy men and virtuous characters as a mark of spiritual refinement.

memes.label: Conquest in the Tales
memes.description: Conquest—the martial career urged upon princes and the plunder gathered by allied kings—frames the military ambition and invasions of the tales.

memes.label: Curse
memes.description: A supernatural sanction laid by sages and deities as punishment for wrongdoing, operating as a major driver of narrative conflict and resolution.

memes.label: Dandaka
memes.description: A forest through which a river flows, on whose bank Siva revealed a sacred rock marked with the footsteps of Gaurí — the site of Matanga's destined kingship.

memes.label: Darpasâra
memes.description: Son of the King of Mâlwa, who performed penance in the mountains and served as regent while his father ruled

memes.label: Death in the Tales
memes.description: Death—whether natural, violent, or judicial—serves as a pivotal narrative event throughout the tales, driving plot reversals and exploring themes of fate and rebirth.

memes.label: Deception in the Tales
memes.description: Deception and cheating—through plausible false stories, financial fraud, and the systematic wiles of ministers—form a persistent motif across the tales.

memes.label: Devotion
memes.description: Sincere religious dedication and personal loyalty, shown through acts of worship at sacred sites and faithful service to gurus, husbands, and deities.

memes.label: Dhanamittra
memes.description: One of the nine companions of Râjavâhana, a wealthy merchant's son renowned for his cleverness and resourcefulness, and owner of a magic purse.

memes.label: Dharmapâla
memes.description: One of Râjahansa's three ministers, father of Kâmapâla and Sumantra, a pious and devoted servant of the crown

memes.label: Dharmavardhana
memes.description: King of the Sravastans, whose character corresponds with his name (protector of righteousness), and father of a very beautiful daughter in Chapter 17.

memes.label: Dhumini
memes.description: Wife of the youngest brother, known for her cunning nature, appearing in the tales of Chapter 18 and 19.

memes.label: Disguise
memes.description: A central narrative device whereby characters conceal or transform their identity to navigate danger, pursue objectives, or test others.

memes.label: Dravidas
memes.description: A country in southern India, the setting for a tale of a young brahman who travelled in search of a suitable wife — told by Mantragupta to a Rakshas.

memes.label: Dream
memes.description: Dreams treated as prophetic visions and reality-blurring experiences, serving as vehicles of divine communication and narrative revelation.

memes.label: Durgâ
memes.description: The goddess Durgâ (also called Kâli), wife of Siva, a powerful deity who appears in visions and protects the faithful

memes.label: Duty
memes.description: Moral obligation and the performance of one's prescribed role, failure of which brings disgrace.

memes.label: Dâruvarma
memes.description: A prince, son of the king's sister, joint regent with Charmavarma, whose unwanted pursuit of Bâlachandrika led to his death

memes.label: Encounter in the Tales
memes.description: Encounters—both hostile and auspicious—punctuate the narrative, from battlefield confrontations to fated meetings with allies and lovers.

memes.label: Escape in the Tales
memes.description: Escapes and flights from danger form a recurring narrative event throughout the tales of the ten princes.

memes.label: Execution in the Tales
memes.description: Judicial execution appears as a recurring narrative event in the tales, where condemned characters face death at the hands of an executioner, often escaping at the last moment.

memes.label: Family
memes.description: The family as the fundamental social unit — a source of duties, obligations, and identity, whose honour and safety characters must protect.

memes.label: Famine in the Tales
memes.description: The great famine recounted in the Tales, which drove men to desperate acts and forced migration to regions of plenty.

memes.label: Fate
memes.description: The power believed to predetermine events and control the course of human lives; in the tales, fate is an all-powerful force that shapes prosperity and adversity alike.

memes.label: Feast in the Tales
memes.description: Feasts and festivals mark the calendar of the narrative world, providing settings for chance encounters, romantic attraction, and communal celebration.

memes.label: Festival of Kâma
memes.description: The great spring festival of Kâma celebrated at Avanti, where Râjavâhana first beheld Avantisundari — the single most consequential event in the Daśakumāracarita, setting the central romance in motion.

memes.label: Forgiveness in the Tales
memes.description: Forgiveness—royal, marital, and divine—recurs as an event that lifts curses, restores broken bonds, and marks moral reconciliation across the tales.

memes.label: Fortune
memes.description: The variable condition of prosperity or adversity in a person's life; in the tales, fortune is an unstable force that can be restored through perseverance, divine intervention, or magical aid.

memes.label: Funeral in the Tales
memes.description: The funeral rites and sati (widow self-immolation) depicted in the Tales, most notably in the Chapter 15 funeral pyre sequence.

memes.label: Ganges
memes.description: The sacred river of India, on whose banks stand the city of Champa and numerous hermitages — a recurring setting for ritual bathing, ascetic practice, and pivotal encounters.

memes.label: Gaurí
memes.description: Goddess, wife of Siva, sister of Kâli, associated with the Dandaka forest.

memes.label: Gomini
memes.description: A woman known for her cunning, cited alongside Dhumini, Ratnavati, and Nitambavati as an example of feminine wiles in Chapter 18.

memes.label: Grihagupta
memes.description: Father of Balabhadra's lawful wife, a resident of Valabhi, mentioned during the legal proceedings in Apahâravarma's narrative.

memes.label: Heaven
memes.description: The divine realm and its inhabitants, invoked as a source of punishment and as the abode of celestial beings (Apsaras).

memes.label: Himâlaya
memes.description: The great mountain range of northern India, a destination of pilgrimage and the setting for encounters with holy rishis. Contains the peak Kailâsa and is the scene of a transformative curse in the narrative.

memes.label: Honour
memes.description: A social value of respect, esteem, and due treatment accorded to worthy persons, marking status and moral standing in the narrative world.

memes.label: Hunt in the Tales
memes.description: Hunting—as royal sport, a husband's laborious duty, and a pretext for ambush—appears across the tales as pastime, toil, and narrative device.

memes.label: Imprisonment in the Tales
memes.description: Instances of imprisonment and captivity in the Tales, including unjust confinement of princes and the dramatic prison-break narrative.

memes.label: Intrigue in the Tales
memes.description: Political and court intrigues—secret schemes, manipulations, and machinations—constitute a recurring event type that drives the power dynamics of the narrative.

memes.label: Invasion in the Tales
memes.description: Foreign invasions—the crossing of borders with hostile intent—appear as decisive narrative events that reshape the political landscape of the tales.

memes.label: Journey in the Tales
memes.description: Journeys by land and sea propel the narrative structure of the tales, scattering the princes across the world and bringing them back together.

memes.label: Justice
memes.description: A principle of righteous judgment and lawful administration, exercised by kings and elders in the narrative as a mark of virtuous rule.

memes.label: Kailâsa
memes.description: A mountain, part of the Himâlaya chain, the abode of Kuvera and source of a messenger's letter from Darpasâra during Chandavarma's siege.

memes.label: Kâlagupta
memes.description: A rich man living in the island of Kâlayavana, father of Suvritta and master of the old nurse who tells the story of Ratnodbhava.

memes.label: "Kalahakantaka"
memes.description: "Kalahakantaka, a man of great strength from Madhura who fell in love with a portrait, disguised himself as a mendicant, and whose actions led to tragic consequences."

memes.label: Kâlayavana
memes.description: Ruler of an island visited by the princes, father of Suvritta, whose daughter married Ratnodbhava.

memes.label: Kalindí
memes.description: Queen of Pâtâla, daughter of a chief of Asuras, and wife of the brahman Matanga.

memes.label: Kalinga
memes.description: A kingdom in eastern India whose king was captured by Mantragupta, and whose daughter Kanakalekhâ became Mantragupta's wife.

memes.label: Kalpasundari
memes.description: Queen of Vikatavarma, the usurper, and later associate of Upahâravarma, appearing in the tale of Chapter 12.

memes.label: Kâma
memes.description: The Hindu god of love and desire, whose annual festival at Avanti brings Râjavâhana and Avantisundari together; also a central philosophical concept in the Daśakumāracarita as one of the three aims of life.

memes.label: Kâmamanjari
memes.description: A clever actress and dancer who seduced the great muni Mârîchi and was involved in the affair of the magic purse

memes.label: Kâmapâla
memes.description: Son of a minister, lord of Benâres, one of whose sons became a companion of Râjavâhana

memes.label: Kanakalekha
memes.description: Princess of Kalinga, daughter of the King of Kalinga, and wife of Mantragupta.

memes.label: Kanakavati
memes.description: Daughter of Niddhipatidatta, whose name is invoked by a false accuser in the legal proceedings against Balabhadra.

memes.label: Kandarpa
memes.description: The God of Love, also known as Kâma, in Hindoo mythology.

memes.label: Kandukavati
memes.description: Princess of Mahishmati who performed the ball-dance to propitiate the goddess Durgâ, sister of Bhimadhanwa

memes.label: Kantaka
memes.description: The gaoler of the King of Magadha, killed by Upahâravarma after making a false accusation against him

memes.label: Kantimati
memes.description: Wife of Kâmapâla and mother of Arthapâla, a princess of the royal household of Magadha

memes.label: Kâvari
memes.description: A river in ancient India where a Brahman disciple found a woman in distress carrying a child — the infant Apahâravarma.

memes.label: Knowledge
memes.description: The possession of information, understanding, and learning; in the tales, knowledge ranges from scholarly erudition to supernatural insight into past and future events.

memes.label: Kosadâsa
memes.description: A merchant and friend to the narrator, lover of Chandrasena, appearing in Chapters 18, 25, and 26.

memes.label: Krittika
memes.description: The constellation of the Pleiades, referenced in the text as a significant astrological marker for timing events.

memes.label: Kulapâlika
memes.description: Daughter of a rich merchant, engaged to Dhanamittra from childhood and central figure in the magic purse affair

memes.label: Kuntala
memes.description: A kingdom in ancient India whose king was persuaded to join the coalition against King Mânasâra of Mâlwa — mentioned in the conquest narrative of Chapter 24.

memes.label: Kuvera
memes.description: The God of Wealth, master of the Yakshas, whose divine intervention shapes the fates of several mortal characters

memes.label: Lakshmí
memes.description: Goddess of fortune, beauty, and prosperity, the lotus-bearing consort of Vishnu.

memes.label: Lâta
memes.description: A region ruled by Lord Mattakâla, whose attempted forced marriage to Vâmalochanâ, daughter of Víraketu, leads to a siege of Pâtali and a conflict with Mânapâla.

memes.label: Madhura
memes.description: A city in ancient India, home to the violent and pleasure-seeking Kalahakantaka — setting of the story of Nitambavati told by Mantragupta.

memes.label: Magadha
memes.description: The central kingdom of the narrative, ruled by King Râjahansa, father of the hero Râjavâhana, with its magnificent capital at Pushpapuri.

memes.label: Magic
memes.description: Supernatural objects and powers that drive key plot developments, including magic weapons, jewels, ointments, and candles.

memes.label: Mahâkâla
memes.description: A famous temple of Siva, the object of many pilgrimages, where devotees perform penance and seek the god's favour. Several key characters in the Daśakumāracarita visit or vow to visit this shrine.

memes.label: Mahishmati
memes.description: A city governed by Satahali, where Arthapâla and his companions arrived safely and gathered intelligence on the political situation.

memes.label: Malaya
memes.description: A mountain range renowned for its sandal groves, whose perfumed wind is celebrated in classical Sanskrit poetry and invoked throughout the Daśakumāracarita as a symbol of romantic longing.

memes.label: Mâlwa
memes.description: An ancient kingdom in central India ruled by King Mânasâra, the antagonist who conquered Magadha and drove Râjahansa from his throne.

memes.label: Mânapâla
memes.description: An officer in the service of the King of Mâlwa, entrusted with guarding the princess Vâmalochanâ

memes.label: Mânasâra
memes.description: King of Mâlwa, the haughty antagonist who conquered Râjahansa and became the father of Avantisundari, wife of Râjavâhana

memes.label: Manibhadra
memes.description: A Yaksha, father of Târâvali, wife of Kâmapâla.

memes.label: Manjuvâdini
memes.description: Princess, elder sister of a young prince, daughter of Anantavarma, who becomes the wife of the narrator (minister) in reward for his services.

memes.label: Mantragupta
memes.description: One of the ten princes, son of a minister of King Râjahansa, who married Ambâlika and Kanakalekha

memes.label: Marriage in the Tales of Ten Princes
memes.description: The recurring event of marriage across the Daśakumāracarita — the unions that bind princes to princesses, seal political alliances, and resolve the narrative arcs of the ten companions.

memes.label: Matanga
memes.description: A brahman sage, apprentice of Vâmadeva, who received a vision from Siva and accompanied Râjavâhana to Pâtâla

memes.label: Mattakâla
memes.description: Lord of Lâta who besieged Pâtali to obtain Vâmalochanâ in marriage.

memes.label: Meditation
memes.description: A spiritual discipline of concentrated contemplation, often practised alongside penance and prayer as a path to religious attainment.

memes.label: Miracle in the Tales
memes.description: Miracles—public demonstrations of supernatural power by holy men and divine agents—serve as moments of revelation and social transformation in the tales.

memes.label: Mithila
memes.description: An ancient kingdom and city in northern India, ruled by King Prahâravarma and later Vikatavarma, home to several of the ten princes and their allies.

memes.label: Mitragupta
memes.description: One of the ten princes, one of the nine companions of Râjavâhana, whose adventures form a separate chapter

memes.label: Music
memes.description: A refined cultural accomplishment — music, along with dancing and singing — marked the cultivation of princes and courtly life.

memes.label: Nâlijangha
memes.description: An old servant rescued by Visruta from a well, who later served as Mantragupta's trusted agent and messenger in the affairs of Mahishmati.

memes.label: Navamâlika
memes.description: Princess of Sravasti, daughter of the King of Sravasti, beloved of Pramati, appearing in the tale of Chapter 16.

memes.label: Niddhipatidatta
memes.description: Father of Kanakavati, mentioned during the legal proceedings against Balabhadra in Apahâravarma's narrative.

memes.label: Nitambavati
memes.description: The wife of a merchant living at Avanti, whose beauty captivated Kalahakantaka and who became an example of feminine cunning.

memes.label: Oath in the Tales
memes.description: Oaths—solemn sworn declarations that bind speaker and listener—function as pivotal speech acts in the tales, creating obligations that drive narrative action.

memes.label: Padmodbhava
memes.description: One of Râjahansa's three ministers, father of Susruta, a steadfast member of the Magadhan council

memes.label: Pâtâla
memes.description: A fabulous subterranean country in Hindu mythology, ruled by King Matanga, where Râjavâhana journeyed and received a magic jewel.

memes.label: Penance
memes.description: A central religious practice involving severe self-discipline and austerity to propitiate the gods and acquire spiritual power.

memes.label: Pilgrimage
memes.description: A journey to a holy place for religious purposes, a recurring practice among Hindoo characters seeking purification and divine favour.

memes.label: Pleasure
memes.description: One of the three great aims of human life in Hindu philosophy, explored throughout the Daśakumāracarita as a force that drives characters toward both fulfillment and folly.

memes.label: Plunder in the Tales
memes.description: Plunder—the violent seizure of goods by bandits, raiders, and conquerors—is a recurring event in the tales that strips characters of resources and redirects their journeys.

memes.label: Poetry
memes.description: A refined literary art — alongside music and drama — that marks the cultivated education of princes and courtiers in the narrative world.

memes.label: Poisoning in the Tales
memes.description: Poisoning appears as a recurring method of intrigue and assassination throughout the tales, employed in court conspiracies and personal vendettas.

memes.label: Possession in the Tales
memes.description: Possession—the seizure of a mortal body by an evil spirit or vetala—recurs as a supernatural affliction across the tales, sometimes inflicted as a curse and sometimes demanding exorcism.

memes.label: "Prachandavarma"
memes.description: "Prachandavarma, Prince and later King or Governor of Mahishmati, younger brother of the king, assassinated in a succession struggle."

memes.label: Prahâravarma
memes.description: King of Mithila, father of Apahâravarma and Upahâravarma, who fought alongside his allies against the King of Mâlwa

memes.label: Pramati
memes.description: One of the ten princes, son of Sumati, whose adventures are paired with those of Arthapâla

memes.label: Prayer
memes.description: The act of addressing the gods with petitions, praises, and offerings, often paired with sacrifice and penance as a core religious practice.

memes.label: Priyamvadâ
memes.description: Queen of Prahâravarma, who lost her sons and whose story involves a marriage engagement

memes.label: Prophecy
memes.description: Supernatural foreknowledge — prophecies delivered by munis and sages drive key narrative arcs through acts of belief, trust, and fulfilment.

memes.label: Punishment in the Tales
memes.description: Punishment—whether divine retribution from heaven or legal sanction by earthly authorities—appears as a significant narrative event reflecting the moral order of the tales.

memes.label: Purnabhadra
memes.description: A reformed robber who became a devoted servant and friend of Kâmapâla, helping him execute the plan to rescue his father Sinhaghosha.

memes.label: Pushpapuri
memes.description: The capital city of Magadha, seat of King Râjahansa, and the birthplace of Râjavâhana — the centre of royal power before Mânasâra's conquest.

memes.label: Pushpodbhava
memes.description: One of the ten princes, a companion of Somadatta who was found as an infant and raised by a village woman

memes.label: Quarrel in the Tales
memes.description: Quarrel—a rupture of accord that ranges from marital rift to engineered factional strife—recurs as the spark that ignites aggression, separation, and political manipulation across the tales.

memes.label: Râjahansa
memes.description: King of Magadha, father of Râjavâhana, the central sovereign figure of the narrative

memes.label: Râjavâhana
memes.description: The hero of the Daśakumāracarita, son of King Râjahansa of Magadha, husband of Avantisundari, and leader of the ten princes whose adventures form the narrative.

memes.label: Râmatirtha
memes.description: A holy pilgrimage site in ancient India, visited by a disciple of Vâmadeva who brought the infant Arthapâla to King Râjahansa.

memes.label: Ratnavati
memes.description: Daughter of a ship-captain of Surat, wife of the merchant Balabhadra, known for her cunning in the elopement story of Chapters 19 and 20.

memes.label: Ratnodbhava
memes.description: A young merchant, son of a minister of the King of Magadha, one of the companions of Râjavâhana, who travelled by land and sea and was reunited with his wife after a shipwreck

memes.label: Rescue in the Tales
memes.description: Acts of rescue—pulling the imperilled from danger—constitute a recurring narrative event that reveals character and advances the interconnected stories of the princes.

memes.label: Revenge in the Tales
memes.description: Revenge—the pursuit of personal vengeance against wrongdoers—drives key narrative episodes across multiple chapters, motivating characters to dramatic action.

memes.label: Robbery in the Tales
memes.description: Robbery—violent theft and plunder—appears as a narrative event reflecting social disorder and providing occasions for displays of cleverness and bravery.

memes.label: Râgamanjari
memes.description: An actress, sister of Kâmamanjari, who made her first appearance as a dancer during the narrative

memes.label: Sacrifice
memes.description: A religious ritual of offering to the gods, ranging from prayers and libations to the solemn performance of human sacrifice.

memes.label: Sagaradatta
memes.description: Mother of the Queen of Oude, daughter of the merchant Vaisravana of Pâtaliputra.

memes.label: Satahali
memes.description: Governor of the district engaged by Mantragupta to bring a false accusation against Anantasíra.

memes.label: Satyavarma
memes.description: A brahman and pilgrim, youngest son of minister Sitavarma, who renounced worldly life and became the father of Somadatta

memes.label: Siege in the Tales
memes.description: The siege of fortified cities is a recurring military event in the tales, with multiple princes and kings laying siege to capitals as acts of war or retribution.

memes.label: Simanta
memes.description: A religious ceremony performed during pregnancy, celebrated with great magnificence in royal households.

memes.label: "Sinhaghosha"
memes.description: "Sinhaghosha, formerly a chief officer of police who became King of Benâres as a boy, later deposed from the throne."

memes.label: Sinhavarma
memes.description: King of Anga, father of Ambâlika, an old friend of Arthapâla who was taken prisoner in battle

memes.label: Sitavarma
memes.description: One of Râjahansa's three ministers, father of Satyavarma, whose youngest son became a companion of the princes

memes.label: Siva
memes.description: The mighty god Siva (Shiva), worshipped at the temple of Mahâkâla, who grants boons to his devotees and appears in visions throughout the narrative

memes.label: Somadatta
memes.description: One of the ten princes, son of Satyavarma and husband of Vâmalochana

memes.label: Spell in the Tales
memes.description: Spells—magical incantations laid upon or removed from characters—function as supernatural narrative devices in the tales, concentrated in the story of Nitambavati.

memes.label: Sravasti
memes.description: A city and kingdom ruled by King Dharmavardhana, visited by Apahâravarma in his search for the actress Râgamanjari.

memes.label: Sringâlika
memes.description: The nurse of Râgamanjari, a quick-witted and resourceful old woman who served as a trusted messenger and ally during the gambler's imprisonment, escape, and courtship of Princess Ambâlika

memes.label: Sumantra
memes.description: Son of Dharmapâla and brother of Kâmapâla and Sumittra.

memes.label: Sumati
memes.description: Minister of King Râjahansa and father of Prince Pramati, one of the ten princes

memes.label: Sumittra
memes.description: Brother of Kâmapâla and Sumantra, son of Dharmapâla, who became the guardian of Prince Arthapâla.

memes.label: Surat
memes.description: A country in ancient India, the homeland of a rich ship-captain whose daughter Ratnavati was married to Balabhadra — told by Apahâravarma in the story of the three answers.

memes.label: Susruta
memes.description: Son of Padmodbhava, one of the companions of the princes who took charge of Pushpodbhava after the shipwreck.

memes.label: Suvritta
memes.description: Daughter of a merchant in the island of Kâlayavana and wife of Ratnodbhava.

memes.label: "Târâvali"
memes.description: "Târâvali, a Yaksha lady, daughter of the chief Yaksha Manibhadra and wife of Kâmapâla, involved in the narrative of Kuvera and the celestial jewel."

memes.label: Theft in the Tales
memes.description: Theft—the clandestine taking of property—appears in the tales as a crime that drives plot through accusation, investigation, and the threat of capital punishment.

memes.label: Torture in the Tales
memes.description: Torture appears as a recurring event in the tales, used both as judicial punishment and as a means of extracting confessions, often testing the fortitude of the accused.

memes.label: Trigarta
memes.description: A country that was home to three wealthy brothers whose story of famine, cannibalism, and escape illustrates the extremity of human suffering — told by Apahâravarma.

memes.label: Truth
memes.description: Truth as an epistemological and moral value — invoked in contexts of testimony, revelation, and the verification of claims.

memes.label: Upahâravarma
memes.description: One of the Ten Princes, a companion of Râjavâhana who was saved from death as an infant and later narrated his story in Chapter 12

memes.label: Vaisravana
memes.description: A wealthy merchant of Pâtaliputra, father of Sagaradatta.

memes.label: Valabhi
memes.description: A city where Grihagupta resides, the father of the lawful wife of a merchant. Valabhi serves as a point of reference in a legal dispute over a woman's identity in Mitragupta's narrative.

memes.label: Vâmadeva
memes.description: A celebrated rishi and holy man who advised King Râjahansa, foretold the birth of Râjavâhana, and sent disciples bearing children to the court

memes.label: Vasantabhânu
memes.description: A rival king whose name is invoked by an assassin to deflect suspicion in Section 24.

memes.label: Vasumati
memes.description: Queen of Magadha, wife of Râjahansa, and mother of Râjavâhana, the hero of the narrative

memes.label: Vasundhara
memes.description: Queen of King Anantavarma of Vidarba, and mother of Manjuvâdini, who acts under the goddess Durgâ's direction in Chapter 24.

memes.label: Vedas
memes.description: The sacred scriptures of the Hindoo tradition, studied by princes and brahmans as the foundation of religious and worldly knowledge.

memes.label: Vidarba
memes.description: A kingdom ruled by King Anantavarma, renowned for wisdom, and the homeland of the narrator prince who recounts his story in Chapter 23.

memes.label: Vidyeswara
memes.description: A famous conjurer who travelled exhibiting his skill for the amusement of kings and used his arts to arrange the marriage of Râjavâhana to Avantisundari

memes.label: Vikatavarma
memes.description: King of Mithila, usurper who rebelled against his uncle Prahâravarma, appearing in the tale of Chapter 12.

memes.label: Vimardaka
memes.description: Keeper of a gaming house employed by Apahâravarma, used as an agent in the schemes against Dhanamittra and Arthapati.

memes.label: Vindhya
memes.description: A mountain range and forest in central India, home to the goddess Durgâ (Vindhyavâsiní) and the setting for several encounters in the adventures of the ten princes.

memes.label: Víraketu
memes.description: King of Pâtali, father of Vâmalochanâ, who betrothed his daughter to Prince Somadatta and declared him his successor

memes.label: Virtue
memes.description: One of the three aims of life in Hindu philosophy, governing righteous conduct and moral duty.

memes.label: Vishnu
memes.description: Vishnu, one of the three principal gods of the Hindu pantheon, who appears in visions to guide characters and is invoked throughout the narrative alongside Brahma and Siva

memes.label: Vision in the Tales
memes.description: Supernatural visions and revelatory dreams serve as narrative devices throughout the tales, guiding characters toward their destinies.

memes.label: Visruta
memes.description: One of the ten princes, son of a minister of King Râjahansa, who rescued Nâlijangha from a well and killed King Prachandavarma of Mahishmati

memes.label: Vâmalochanâ
memes.description: Daughter of Víraketu, King of Pâtali, renowned for her great beauty, and wife of Prince Somadatta

memes.label: Wealth
memes.description: One of the three aims of life in Hindu philosophy, representing material prosperity and economic well-being.

memes.label: Wedding in the Tales
memes.description: Weddings and marriage ceremonies recur throughout the tales, serving as occasions for political alliance, romantic fulfilment, and narrative complication.

memes.label: Wisdom
memes.description: The quality of having experience, knowledge, and good judgment; a prized virtue in the world of the tales, associated with kingship and right conduct.

memes.label: Worship
memes.description: The act of religious devotion and reverence directed toward deities; a central ritual practice in the world of the tales, performed at temples, rivers, and sacred sites.

memes.label: Yama
memes.description: God and Judge of the Infernal Regions, the deity of death and justice in the narrative's mythological framework
